payload.text:
Development of a Smart Course Material Recommendation System (Low-Code)

Introduction: Students often waste time searching for suitable academic materials.
Purpose: To recommend resources based on course title and topic.
Problem Statement: Lack of centralized intelligent search for course materials.
Aim/Objectives: Categorize materials; design a recommendation workflow.
Methodology: Data classification, simple rule-based recommendation logic implemented in low-code platforms like Airtable/Notion, user feedback.
Expected Result: A lightweight material suggestion system requiring minimal coding

**Defining the Scope**

I'm currently focused on defining the core features of this "Smart Course Material Recommendation System." I've nailed down the essentials: a solid course search, topic-based recommendations, smart material categorization, and a crucial user feedback loop. Now, I'm working to expand the definition of each feature and prioritize. The tech stack is next, but I'm thinking about the user experience first.


**Outlining the Tech**

I've sketched out the initial tech stack: React with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS for the front-end, the Gemini API for recommendations, and Recharts/D3 for data visualization. I'm considering a low-code approach, perhaps mimicking an Airtable/Notion interface, but with AI-powered features. I'm focusing on the search bar, recommendation engine powered by Gemini, a dashboard view for categorized materials, and a feedback system. I'm also planning a clean, academic UI. I've also outlined the initial file structure, starting with the index file, the main app file and type files, and a file for the Gemini service.


**Detailing the Components**

I've sketched out the crucial components: `Layout`, `MaterialCard`, `RecommendationEngine`, `MaterialDashboard`, and `StatsSection`. I'm integrating Gemini's `gemini-3-flash-preview` with JSON response for structured data. I'm also preparing mock data for the initial setup. I'll use a `HashRouter` for simple navigation. Styling is with Tailwind, with an indigo/slate palette.

**Analyzing App.tsx Structure**

I'm currently structuring the `App.tsx` component for the "Smart Course Material Recommendation System." Right now, I'm focusing on the dashboard and search view, ensuring I include input fields for "Course Title," "Topic," and "Level." I am planning how to handle the input and the interactions. The "Level" field will have predefined options. Next is how to proceed with the 'Get Recommendations' button.

**Diagnosing a Syntax Error**

This truncation seems to be the culprit behind the syntax error, as it leaves the code syntactically incorrect. I believe identifying the root cause should be the next step.

entries.description: Fix the SyntaxError by providing the complete, non-truncated implementation of the App component, including Discovery, Library, and Analytics views.

import React, { useState, useMemo } from 'react'; import { Search, Library, BarChart3, GraduationCap, ChevronRight, BookOpen, ExternalLink, Sparkles, Filter, Loader2, TrendingUp, Award, BookMarked } from 'lucide-react'; import { RecommendationRequest, Material, MaterialCategory } from './types'; import { getAIRecommendations } from './services/gemini'; import { BarChart, Bar, XAxis, YAxis, CartesianGrid, Tooltip, ResponsiveContainer, PieChart, Pie, Cell } from 'recharts'; const COLORS = ['#6366f1', '#8b5cf6', '#ec4899', '#f43f5e', '#f97316']; const App: React.FC = () => { const [activeTab, setActiveTab] = useState<'recommend' | 'library' | 'analytics'>('recommend'); const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false); const [form, setForm] = useState({ courseTitle: '', topic: '', level: 'Beginner' }); const [results, setResults] = useState<{ summary: string; recommendations: Material[] } | null>(null); // Initialize with some mock data for better first impression const [library, setLibrary] = useState([ { id: 'init-1', title: 'Introduction to Algorithms', author: 'Thomas H. Cormen', category: MaterialCategory.BOOK, url: 'https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262046305/introduction-to-algorithms/', description: 'A comprehensive guide to algorithm design and analysis.', relevanceScore: 98, tags: ['Computer Science', 'Algorithms'] }, { id: 'init-2', title: 'CS50: Introduction to Computer Science', author: 'David J. Malan', category: MaterialCategory.COURSE, url: 'https://www.edx.org/course/introduction-computer-science-harvardx-cs50x', description: 'Harvard University\'s introduction to the intellectual enterprises of computer science.', relevanceScore: 95, tags: ['CS50', 'Programming'] } ]); const handleRecommend = async (e: React.FormEvent) => { e.preventDefault(); if (!form.courseTitle || !form.topic) return; setLoading(true); try { const data = await getAIRecommendations(form); setResults(data); // Update library with new unique recommendations setLibrary(prev => { const existingIds = new Set(prev.map(p => p.id)); const newOnes = data.recommendations.filter(r => !existingIds.has(r.id)); return [...newOnes, ...prev]; }); } catch (error) { console.error("Failed to fetch recommendations:", error); } finally { setLoading(false); } }; const analyticsData = useMemo(() => { const categoryCounts = library.reduce((acc, item) => { acc[item.category] = (acc[item.category] || 0) + 1; return acc; }, {} as Record); const pieData = Object.entries(categoryCounts).map(([name, value]) => ({ name, value })); const barData = library.slice(0, 6).map(item => ({ name: item.title.length > 12 ? item.title.substring(0, 12) + '...' : item.title, score: item.relevanceScore })); return { pieData, barData }; }, [library]); const MaterialCard = ({ material }: { material: Material }) => (
{material.category}
{material.relevanceScore}%

{material.title}

By {material.author}

{material.description}

{material.tags.map(tag => ( #{tag} ))}
View Resource
); return (
{/* Sidebar */}
EduStream

AI Enabled

Gemini 3 Turbo is analyzing your curriculum

System Ready
{/* Main Content */}

{activeTab === 'recommend' && 'Discovery Engine'} {activeTab === 'library' && 'Personal Collection'} {activeTab === 'analytics' && 'Performance Metrics'}

JD
{activeTab === 'recommend' && (

Personalized Learning Reimagined.

Tell us what you're studying, and we'll curate a custom syllabus of high-impact materials.

setForm({...form, courseTitle: e.target.value})} required />
setForm({...form, topic: e.target.value})} required />
{results && (

Curated Recommendations

Expert Reasoning

"{results.summary}"

{results.recommendations.map(m => ( ))}
)}
)} {activeTab === 'library' && (

Resource Repository

Your saved and previously discovered academic materials.

{library.length > 0 ? (
{library.map((m, idx) => ( ))}
) : (

Your library is empty

Discover high-quality materials to start building your personalized academic archive.

)}
)} {activeTab === 'analytics' && (

Total Discovery

{library.length} +15%

Avg Relevance

{library.length > 0 ? (library.reduce((a, b) => a + b.relevanceScore, 0) / library.length).toFixed(1) : '0'}% High Quality

Learning Pace

Steady Active

Material Types

{library.length > 0 ? ( {analyticsData.pieData.map((entry, index) => ( ))} ) : (
No analysis data available
)}

Top Scores

{library.length > 0 ? ( ) : (
No analysis data available
)}
)}
); }; export default App;

diagnostics.message:
Type '{ key: any; material: any; }' is not assignable to type '{ material: Material; }'.
Property 'key' does not exist on type '{ material: Material; }'.

Type '{ key: string; material: any; }' is not assignable to type '{ material: Material; }'.
Property 'key' does not exist on type '{ material: Material; }'.

payload.text:
**Addressing the Errors**

I'm currently focused on the `App.tsx` file, specifically line 283. I'm trying to resolve a type mismatch, ensuring the object assignment adheres to the expected `{ material: Material; }` type. I'm also ensuring the Gemini API usage complies with all stipulated guidelines.


**Investigating the 'key' Prop**

I've homed in on the `MaterialCard` component, and am tracing the use of the `key` prop, specifically where the error is triggered. I've re-examined the props definition of `MaterialCard`, which seems to not include `key` in the `MaterialCard` component itself, and how it is used across the codebase, particularly on lines 283 and 306. It appears I'm passing `key` correctly, so I must be missing something in the type definitions or React's handling of the `key` prop.


**Refining the Component Definition**

I'm now diving deeper into how `MaterialCard` is defined, and I'm considering TS's interpretation of it as a standard React component. I'm focusing on why it might not be recognizing the `key` prop correctly. The error points to a strict type check for `{ material: Material }`. I'm going to look into `React.FC<{ material: Material }>` or adjusting the destructuring. The simplest solution might be ensuring that `MaterialCard` is defined such that it implicitly accepts `key`. I'll also be reviewing the updated Gemini API guidelines.


**Reviewing Gemini Integration**

I've confirmed the Gemini API setup in `services/gemini.ts`. The import of `@google/genai` is fine. Now, I'm refactoring the initialization to use `process.env.API_KEY` directly, as per the guidelines. I'm also going to move the system instruction into the `config` object of the `generateContent` call. All other usages appear correct.


**Finalizing the Gemini Configuration**

I've streamlined the Gemini API integration. I've switched to utilizing `process.env.API_KEY` directly within the function call. The `systemInstruction` has been relocated to `config.systemInstruction`. I'm making sure `response.text` is correctly accessed for proper data retrieval.

**Addressing React Key Prop**

I'm zeroing in on resolving the "key" prop issue in `MaterialCard`. The type definition of `MaterialCard` is causing the conflict. I'm going to transition `MaterialCard` to use `React.FC<{ material: Material }>` or explicitly include `key` in the prop type. The goal is to get React to properly handle the key rather than TS throwing a fuss.


**Incorporating Key Prop**

I'm now implementing `React.FC<{ material: Material }>` for `MaterialCard`. This seems the best way to leverage React's built-in handling of the `key` prop. I'm also going to check `services/gemini.ts` to see why the API key and prompt aren't configured correctly.


**Refactoring the Gemini Prompt**

I've just moved the system instruction from the prompt itself in `gemini.ts` to `config.systemInstruction` within the `generateContent` call. I've confirmed that the model is specified as `gemini-3-flash-preview` as per guidelines, and I am double checking the errors in `App.tsx` related to the `key` prop, noting a type mismatch issue on line 283 and 306. The prompts are now handled as expected.


**Updating MaterialCard Type**

I'm now changing `MaterialCard` to `React.FC<{ material: Material }>`, resolving the "key" prop type issue. This should allow React to handle the `key` prop correctly without TypeScript errors. I've also verified the Gemini API is correctly configured in `gemini.ts`. I also checked the safety of the `response.text` getter.


**Verifying the API Key**

**Adjusting Prop Types**

Instead of `React.FC<{ material: Material }>`, I'm going to explicitly include `key?: string | number` within the prop definition. This should make TS happy since it is validating the component's props against `{ material: Material }`. I will focus on the simplest solution first.

I'm now implementing the `React.FC<{ material: Material }>` approach for `MaterialCard`, which should be able to resolve the key prop conflict without issues. I'll test it out to ensure the type error is gone. I'm also reviewing `App.tsx` and updating the model name in the sidebar from "Gemini 3 Turbo" to "Gemini 3 Flash". This should align with the current Google AI guidelines.



**Validating Data Structures**

I'm now cross-referencing `MaterialCategory` in `App.tsx` with the enum in `gemini.ts` to ensure consistency. It seems the enum values `'Video'`, `'Article'`, `'Book'`, `'Podcast'`, `'Course'` match the enum definition, and it appears the data types are matching. All errors have now been resolved.


**Verifying Type Compatibility**

I've confirmed that the enum values used for `MaterialCategory` are consistent between `gemini.ts` and `App.tsx`, avoiding any type mismatch issues. I've double-checked the state initialization using `useState` in `App.tsx`, and the type matches `AIRecommendationResponse`. The key issue appears to be related to TypeScript and React's handling of the `key` prop, specifically in how a local function component is typed. The error arises because TypeScript expects a `FunctionalComponent` for proper JSX support.


**Validating Gemini Output**

I'm now comparing the expected response format with the actual output of the Gemini API. The structure appears correct so far: a `summary` string and a `recommendations` array containing objects. Each recommendation object includes an `id`, `title`, and `description`, along with other specific fields and a `MaterialCategory` enum for the `category`. I'm verifying the types used for tags using `Type.ARRAY` in `gemini.ts`. I am happy with the current output.

My goal is to use this simple definition to resolve the lingering type mismatch, confirming React is properly using `key`. I'm also reviewing `App.tsx` and verifying all necessary imports.

entries.description: Refactor getAIRecommendations to comply with Google GenAI SDK guidelines: use process.env.API_KEY directly, move system instructions to config, and handle response.text correctly.
Fix TypeScript errors by typing MaterialCard as a React.FC and update model name in the UI for accuracy.

payload.text: I want to view the website on a mobile phone and also make it compulsory for user to register first before the can access the materials

**Prioritizing Mobile First**

I'm currently focused on ensuring EduStream functions flawlessly on mobile. My immediate task involves adding a registration prompt. I am working through the `App.tsx` file to incorporate this. I've been considering how this will be presented on smaller screens.

entries.description: Implemented mobile-responsive design and a mandatory registration screen. Added responsive sidebar navigation for mobile views and optimized all grids/paddings for small screens.

payload.text: I meant the person should be able to create an account, add a username Gmail and password before the can use the website

**Defining Registration Process**

I'm currently focused on defining the registration process. I'm thinking mandatory username, email (Gmail), and password fields are necessary before accessing core features. Making sure the user experience is mobile-friendly is key.


**Refining Registration Interface**

I'm now refining the registration UI, starting with the UserProfile interface to include username and password. I'm expanding the registration form state to accommodate these fields, adding basic validation for a Gmail email address, and ensuring a smooth UI transition to the main dashboard. Mobile responsiveness remains a priority. I'm aiming for a more "compulsory" and professional look. I've updated App.tsx to include full registration state and a "Sign Up" form, focusing on storing credentials in local state during the session. The Registration form now has Username, Email (Gmail validated), and Password fields. The design prioritizes a clean, centralized card with high contrast and modern aesthetics.

entries.description: Implemented a comprehensive authentication system with a mandatory 'Sign Up' and 'Login' flow. Added fields for Username, Gmail (with validation), and Password. Retained and improved mobile responsiveness and the core recommendation functionality.

payload.text: Remove the "study goal" in the sign up page

**Removing the Study Goal**

I'm currently focused on removing the "study goal" field from the sign-up flow. The `UserProfile` interface and `registrationForm` state in `App.tsx` both currently include a `goal` property, which I will remove. Also, I've started checking `handleAuth`.

entries.description: Remove the "study goal" field from the sign-up form and the user profile interface.

payload.text: Add a feature so that the can be able to rate how useful the tool is

**Designing Feedback Mechanisms**

I'm currently focused on incorporating a "rate the tool" feature into EduStream. It seems like a simple rating system with a comment field is the best path. I'm exploring UI options for the rating section, considering stars or a numerical scale for the rating input.


**Refining Interface Location**

I've decided to integrate the feedback mechanism as a "Rate EduStream" section in the sidebar, or potentially a modal triggered after a recommendation. I've also thought about a feedback component in the "Analytics" or "Discovery" tabs. The prompt is about the tool, so focusing on the sidebar or a prompt makes sense.

entries.description: Added a 'Rate the tool' feature in the sidebar and a thank-you state upon submission. The rating is also reflected in the Insights/Analytics dashboard.
